name: Business Plot
description: The Business Plot, also known as the Wall Street Putsch, was an alleged political conspiracy in 1933 in the United States to overthrow President Franklin D. Roosevelt. Retired Marine Corps Major General Smedley Butler claimed that wealthy businessmen were plotting to create a fascist dictatorship and install him as a military dictator. Butler testified before the McCormack–Dickstein Congressional committee, which investigated the allegations. The committee's final report stated that while some testimony was credible, no evidence was found to support the existence of a plot. Mainstream historians generally dismiss the Business Plot as either a fringe theory or a greatly exaggerated account of discontent among some businessmen during the early years of the New Deal. Proponents argue that the committee's findings were a whitewash, pointing to circumstantial evidence and the reluctance of alleged conspirators to testify. Critics emphasize the lack of concrete evidence and inconsistencies in Butler's account, suggesting that he may have misinterpreted or embellished the situation. The incident remains a controversial topic, with ongoing debate about the extent and seriousness of the alleged plot.
subtitle: Alleged Fascist Coup Attempt in 1933

claims.claim: Wealthy businessmen plotted to overthrow FDR.
claims.criticism: Lack of corroborating evidence and inconsistencies in Butler's story.

claims.claim: The plot aimed to establish a fascist dictatorship in the US.
claims.evidence: Butler's claims of being offered leadership of a new army.
claims.criticism: Implausibility of a successful coup given the political climate and military loyalty.

claims.claim: The McCormack-Dickstein Committee covered up the true extent of the conspiracy.
claims.evidence: Discrepancies in the committee's report and alleged influence of powerful figures.
claims.criticism: The committee did investigate and found no verifiable evidence of a plot; alternative explanations exist for alleged discrepancies.

keyFigures.name: Smedley Butler
keyFigures.role: Whistleblower who testified about the plot

keyFigures.name: Arthur M. Schlesinger Jr.
keyFigures.role: Historian who downplayed the plot's significance

sources.author: Jules Archer
sources.work: The Plot to Seize the White House
sources.title: Jules Archer

worshipPractices.name: Vigilant Skepticism
worshipPractices.description: Followers engage in constant questioning of established power structures, particularly those involving wealth and influence. This involves active research and dissemination of information to expose potential corruption or conspiracies. The goal is to prevent similar events by maintaining awareness and critical thinking.

worshipPractices.name: Transparency Ritual
worshipPractices.description: Followers actively promote transparency in government and business. This involves advocating for open records, campaign finance reform, and whistleblower protections. The practice is believed to ward off the darkness of hidden agendas and unchecked power.

festivals.name: The Unmasking Day
festivals.timing: Annually on the anniversary of Butler's testimony.
festivals.description: A day dedicated to uncovering hidden truths and exposing corruption. It involves public forums, investigative journalism, and artistic expressions that challenge established narratives and celebrate acts of whistleblowing.

festivals.name: The Roosevelt Remembrance
festivals.description: A day to reflect on the fragility of democracy and the importance of safeguarding against authoritarianism. It usually involves discussions and learning about the New Deal and the challenges Roosevelt faced, with a focus on vigilance against similar threats in the present.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Business Plot serves as a cautionary tale about the potential for corporate influence to undermine democratic institutions. Its legacy lives on in conspiracy theories, political thrillers, and investigative journalism that explores the dark side of wealth and power. It is frequently invoked in discussions about campaign finance reform and the influence of lobbyists.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A plot by aristocrats, led by Catiline, to overthrow the Roman Republic, reflecting the desire for power among elites and their willingness to subvert the existing government for personal gain.

Loki's constant schemes and manipulations to undermine the Aesir gods mirrors the Business Plot's intent to destabilize the government, driven by personal ambition and resentment of the established order.

The Thirty Tyrants, installed in Athens after the Peloponnesian War, represent a small group seizing power and imposing an oppressive regime, similar to the alleged goal of the Business Plot to establish a fascist dictatorship.

Kamsa, a tyrannical king, attempted to kill Krishna due to a prophecy of his overthrow, reflecting the fear and paranoia of those in power and their willingness to use violence and conspiracy to maintain control.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The serpent represents deceit, betrayal, and hidden agendas, reflecting the clandestine nature of the alleged plot.

associations.name: Hubris
associations.significance: The alleged plotters' belief in their own power and ability to control the government mirrors the mythological concept of hubris, leading to a potential downfall.

associations.name: Shadow
associations.significance: Shadow symbolizes the hidden motives and dark dealings behind the conspiracy, operating outside the light of public scrutiny.

associations.name: Eclipse
associations.significance: An eclipse represents a temporary obscuring of the rightful power, mirroring the attempt to overthrow the legitimate president.

associations.name: Thirteen
associations.significance: Thirteen is often associated with bad luck and conspiracies, reflecting the ill-fated nature of the alleged plot.

associations.name: Usurpation
associations.significance: The Business Plot embodies the concept of usurpation, seeking to seize power illegitimately from the rightful leader.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black represents secrecy, evil intentions, and the hidden nature of the conspiracy against the government.

companions.name: Ianus
companions.description: As the Roman god of beginnings, transitions, and endings, Ianus (Janus) would represent the shifting allegiances and secret agendas inherent in the Business Plot, guarding those who seek to expose it.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: The all-seeing giant from Greek mythology, Argus's hundred eyes would be invaluable in uncovering the conspirators and their clandestine activities, a constant watchful presence for those investigating the plot.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: Hermes, the Greek messenger god, could provide swift communication and intelligence gathering, helping to disseminate information about the plot before it could succeed.

companions.name: The Moirai
companions.description: The Greek Fates, Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos, could represent the inevitable unraveling of the plot due to its inherent flaws and the predetermined course of history.

companions.name: Gjallarhorn
companions.description: The Gjallarhorn, Heimdall's horn in Norse mythology, could serve as an early warning system, alerting those opposing the plot to impending dangers and coordinated attacks.

extendedContent.title: The Alleged Conspiracy: Details and Key Players
extendedContent.content:
The Business Plot, as alleged by Major General Smedley Butler, centered around a purported plan to overthrow President Franklin D. Roosevelt in 1933. Butler testified before a special House committee (McCormack-Dickstein Committee) that he had been approached by Gerald MacGuire, a bond salesman with connections to Grayson M-P. Murphy & Co., and other unnamed wealthy individuals. MacGuire allegedly offered Butler the leadership of a 'fascist army' of 500,000 men, promising substantial financial backing. The goal, according to Butler, was to pressure Roosevelt into adopting policies more favorable to big business or, failing that, to replace him with a figurehead controlled by the conspirators. 

The plot supposedly involved creating a veterans' organization led by Butler, leveraging his immense popularity among veterans of World War I. This organization would then march on Washington D.C., ostensibly to demand early payment of a promised bonus to veterans. The ensuing chaos would provide the pretext for a military coup, installing Butler as the dictator. The alleged conspirators included prominent figures like Robert Sterling Clark, a wealthy heir, and representatives from major corporations such as DuPont and J.P. Morgan. However, none of these individuals were ever charged with any crime.

The McCormack-Dickstein Committee ultimately concluded that Butler's testimony was credible, but they found no evidence to support the existence of a fully formed conspiracy or the involvement of the individuals named by Butler. Their report stated that 'attempts to establish such a movement were discussed, were planned, and might have been placed in execution if and when financial backers deemed it expedient.' This ambiguous wording has fueled debate and speculation about the true extent of the Business Plot ever since.

extendedContent.title: Smedley Butler: The Reluctant Whistleblower
extendedContent.content:
Smedley Butler, a two-time Medal of Honor recipient and a highly respected figure among veterans, played a pivotal role in bringing the alleged Business Plot to light. Butler, often referred to as 'The Fighting Quaker,' was known for his outspokenness and his progressive views, particularly concerning the treatment of veterans. His disillusionment with the military and big business stemmed from his experiences in numerous military interventions, which he later denounced as 'racketeering' for the benefit of corporate interests. His 1935 book, 'War is a Racket,' became a seminal text for anti-war activists.

It was this existing anti-establishment sentiment that, according to Butler's testimony, made him receptive to the initial approaches from MacGuire. However, as the alleged plot unfolded and the true intentions became clearer, Butler grew increasingly concerned. He ultimately chose to expose the scheme, despite the potential risks to his reputation and safety. His decision to testify before the McCormack-Dickstein Committee, and his willingness to publicly denounce the alleged conspirators, cemented his image as a champion of the common man and a critic of corporate power.

Butler's credibility, due to his military record and outspoken nature, was a significant factor in the investigation of the Business Plot. While the committee's findings were inconclusive regarding the broader conspiracy, Butler's testimony undoubtedly raised awareness about the potential for wealthy elites to undermine democratic institutions during times of economic crisis. His actions, regardless of the plot's veracity, solidified his legacy as a principled figure who stood up against perceived injustice, even when facing powerful adversaries.

extendedContent.title: Historical Context: The Great Depression and Political Instability
extendedContent.content:
The Business Plot emerged during the depths of the Great Depression, a period of immense economic hardship and social unrest in the United States. The unemployment rate soared to unprecedented levels, banks failed, and widespread poverty gripped the nation. President Franklin D. Roosevelt's New Deal programs, aimed at providing relief, recovery, and reform, faced fierce opposition from some sectors of the business community who viewed them as socialist and detrimental to free enterprise. These anxieties created a fertile ground for extremist ideologies and political conspiracies.

Fascism was on the rise in Europe, with leaders like Benito Mussolini in Italy and Adolf Hitler in Germany consolidating power. Some wealthy Americans, disillusioned with the perceived failures of capitalism and democracy, saw fascism as a potential solution to the economic crisis and social unrest. The alleged Business Plot, whether real or imagined, reflected this climate of fear and uncertainty, and the willingness of some individuals to consider radical alternatives to the existing political order.

The New Deal itself was a massive intervention in the economy, and naturally generated opposition. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for instance, was created in 1934 to regulate the stock market and prevent the kinds of abuses that had contributed to the crash of 1929. Actions like these were viewed by some business leaders as an overreach of government power. Therefore, the historical context of economic crisis, the rise of fascism, and the contentious political landscape of the New Deal are crucial to understanding the environment in which the Business Plot allegedly took place.

extendedContent.title: Significance and Legacy: Conspiracy Theories and Historical Debate
extendedContent.content:
The Business Plot remains a controversial and debated topic in American history. While the McCormack-Dickstein Committee acknowledged the existence of discussions about a potential coup, they stopped short of confirming the existence of a fully formed conspiracy or identifying the individuals named by Butler as participants. This ambiguity has fueled numerous conspiracy theories and historical debates about the true extent of the plot and the motivations of those allegedly involved. Some historians argue that the Business Plot was a genuine threat to American democracy, while others dismiss it as a fabrication or an exaggeration by Butler.

The lack of definitive evidence and the ambiguous findings of the congressional committee make it difficult to definitively prove or disprove the existence of the Business Plot. However, the allegations raised by Butler served as a cautionary tale about the potential for wealthy elites to undermine democratic institutions and the importance of vigilance in safeguarding against such threats. The incident also highlighted the power of conspiracy theories to capture the public imagination, particularly during times of crisis and uncertainty. 

The Business Plot continues to be cited in discussions about the relationship between big business, government, and the military, and the potential for anti-democratic forces to emerge during times of economic and political upheaval. It serves as a reminder of the fragility of democratic institutions and the importance of transparency and accountability in government and business. The story also raises questions about the role of whistleblowers in exposing wrongdoing and the challenges they face in bringing powerful individuals and institutions to account.
